metadata.source: Prabhakaran et al. 2026 Guideline for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke
metadata.publisher: American Heart Association / American Stroke Association
metadata.disclaimer: This tool surfaces recommendations from the AHA/ASA 2026 guideline for clinical reference. It is decision support, not decision making. The treating clinician retains full responsibility for patient care decisions.

2a.label: Class 2a
2a.verb: Is reasonable / Can be useful / Can be beneficial

2b.label: Class 2b
2b.verb: May be reasonable / May be considered / Usefulness uncertain

3-no-benefit.label: Class 3: No Benefit
3-no-benefit.verb: Is not recommended / Is not indicated

3-harm.label: Class 3: Harm
3-harm.verb: Should not be performed / Causes harm

loe_definitions.A: High-quality evidence from >1 RCT, meta-analyses of high-quality RCTs
loe_definitions.B-R: Moderate-quality evidence from ≥1 RCT or meta-analyses of moderate-quality RCTs
loe_definitions.B-NR: Moderate-quality evidence from ≥1 well-designed nonrandomized studies, observational studies, or registry studies
loe_definitions.C-LD: Limited data — randomized or nonrandomized observational/registry studies with limitations of design or execution
loe_definitions.C-EO: Expert opinion — consensus of expert opinion based on clinical experience

recommendations.title: Emergent NCCT or MRI on initial evaluation
recommendations.text: In patients with suspected AIS, emergent brain imaging with NCCT or MRI is recommended on initial evaluation to assess ischemic burden (eg, ASPECTS) and exclude intracranial hemorrhage before initiating reperfusion interventions.

recommendations.title: Emergent imaging within 25 minutes
recommendations.text: In hospital systems that care for patients with suspected AIS, protocols based on process improvement initiatives should be established so that emergent brain imaging can be performed as rapidly as possible (eg, within 25 minutes) to facilitate timely reperfusion interventions.
recommendations.key_point: Target: imaging within 25 minutes of arrival.

recommendations.title: Don't delay vascular imaging for serum creatinine
recommendations.text: In patients with suspected AIS and LVO, emergent vascular imaging with contrast-enhanced CTA and/or CTP should not be delayed to obtain serum creatinine concentration.
recommendations.key_point: Don't wait for creatinine — proceed with CTA/CTP.

recommendations.title: DWI-FLAIR mismatch for unknown-onset/wake-up stroke
recommendations.text: In patients with suspected AIS who awaken from symptoms or have unknown time of onset >4.5 hours from last known well, but are otherwise eligible for thrombolysis, MRI DWI-FLAIR mismatch selection can be useful to determine eligibility for extended window IVT.

recommendations.title: Perfusion imaging for 4.5–24h extended window IVT
recommendations.text: In patients with suspected AIS who awaken with symptoms or have unknown time of onset 4.5 to 24 hours from last known well, CTP or MR DWI-PWI (perfusion-weighted imaging) selection with automated postprocessing software analysis can be useful to determine eligibility for extended window IVT.

recommendations.title: Emergent brain and vascular imaging within 24h of LVO
recommendations.text: In patients with suspected AIS and LVO presenting within 24 hours of last known well, emergent brain and vascular imaging (CT/CTA or MRI/MRA) of the cervical and intracranial vessels should be performed as rapidly as possible for EVT selection and treatment planning.

recommendations.title: Adjunctive perfusion imaging in 6–24h LVO window
recommendations.text: In patients with suspected AIS and LVO presenting within 6 to 24 hours of last known well, adjunctive CTP, MRI (DWI-FLAIR mismatch), or MR DWI-PWI with automated postprocessing software analysis can be useful in the evaluation for EVT, if immediately available.

recommendations.title: Direct triage to angiography (DTAS) from prehospital LVO
recommendations.text: In patients with suspected AIS and LVO based on prehospital assessment with a validated stroke severity scale (eg, RACE >4) and eligible for EVT, direct triage to the angiography suite (DTAS) for flat-panel head CT versus conventional imaging workflow followed by catheter-based angiography may be considered to reduce time to intervention and improve functional outcomes.

recommendations.title: Faster IVT improves functional outcomes
recommendations.text: In adult patients with AIS with disabling deficits (regardless of NIHSS score) and eligible for IVT, faster treatment improves functional outcomes.
recommendations.key_point: Time is brain — every minute of delay matters, regardless of NIHSS.

recommendations.title: Initiate IVT ASAP within 4.5h — don't delay for advanced imaging
recommendations.text: In adult patients with AIS who are eligible for IVT within 4.5 hours of symptom onset, treatment should be initiated as quickly as possible, assuring safe administration and avoiding potential delays associated with additional multimodal neuroimaging, such as CTA/MRA, and CT/MR perfusion imaging.
recommendations.key_point: Don't delay IVT to obtain CTA/CTP — NCCT alone is sufficient for most decisions.

recommendations.title: Check blood glucose before IVT
recommendations.text: In patients with suspected ischemic stroke, treating health care professionals should determine blood glucose levels before IVT initiation to assess and urgently treat severe hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia, which may mimic acute stroke presentations.
recommendations.key_point: Severe hypo/hyperglycemia (<50 or >400 mg/dL) can mimic stroke.

recommendations.title: Treat with IVT despite mild-to-moderate early ischemic changes
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS who are otherwise eligible for IVT with early ischemic change of mild to moderate extent (other than frank hypodensity attributable to the clinical presentation) on initial brain imaging, IVT is recommended to improve functional outcome.

recommendations.title: IVT not recommended for mild, non-disabling deficits within 4.5h
recommendations.text: In eligible adult patients with AIS presenting with mild non-disabling stroke deficits (eg, isolated sensory syndrome in many cases) within 4.5 hours of symptom onset or last known well, IVT is not recommended as it has not shown superiority in improving functional outcomes compared to double antiplatelet treatment.
recommendations.key_point: DAPT is non-inferior to IVT in mild, non-disabling stroke. Use disabling-deficit framework, not NIHSS alone.

recommendations.title: IVT recommended despite single/dual antiplatelet use
recommendations.text: In suspected patients with AIS who are taking single or DAPT and are otherwise eligible for IVT, IVT is recommended to improve functional outcomes despite an increase in risk of sICH compared with no antiplatelet therapy.
recommendations.key_point: Increased absolute sICH risk (~0.9–1.2%) outweighed by treatment benefit (~8%).

recommendations.title: Don't delay IVT for hematology/coagulation testing if no abnormality suspected
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS within 4.5 hours of last known well and eligible for IVT, it is reasonable that IVT not be delayed while waiting for hematologic or coagulation testing if there is no reason to suspect an abnormal result.

recommendations.title: Don't obtain MRI to exclude microbleeds before IVT
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS who are eligible for IVT within 4.5 hours of symptom onset with unknown burden of cerebral microbleeds (CMB), it is recommended that IVT be administered without first obtaining MRI to exclude CMBs.

recommendations.title: Tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg or alteplase 0.9 mg/kg within 4.5h
recommendations.text: In adult patients with AIS presenting within 4.5 hours of symptom onset or last known well and eligible for IVT, tenecteplase at a dose of 0.25 mg/kg body weight (max 25 mg) or alteplase at a dose of 0.9 mg/kg body weight is recommended to improve functional outcomes.
recommendations.key_point: Tenecteplase: single bolus over 5–10s. Alteplase: 10% bolus + 60-min infusion. Pediatric dosing not determined.

recommendations.title: Tenecteplase 0.4 mg/kg dose is NOT recommended
recommendations.text: In adult patients with AIS presenting within 4.5 hours of symptom onset or last known well and eligible for IVT, tenecteplase at a dose of 0.4 mg/kg body weight is not recommended.
recommendations.key_point: Higher tenecteplase dose has shown potential for harm without additional benefit.

recommendations.title: Wake-up/unknown-onset IVT with MRI DWI-FLAIR mismatch
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS who (a) have unknown time of onset and are within 4.5 hours from symptom recognition and (b) have an MRI-DWI lesion smaller than one-third of the MCA territory and no marked signal change on FLAIR, IVT administered within 4.5 hours of stroke symptom recognition can be beneficial to improve functional outcomes.

recommendations.title: Perfusion-selected IVT in 4.5–9h window
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS who have salvageable ischemic penumbra detected on automated perfusion imaging and who (a) awake with stroke symptoms within 9 hours from the midpoint of sleep or (b) are 4.5–9 hours from last known well, IV thrombolysis may be reasonable to improve functional outcomes.

recommendations.title: IVT for LVO in 4.5–24h window when EVT not available
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS due to LVO with salvageable ischemic penumbra, presenting within 4.5 to 24 hours from symptom onset or last known well, and who cannot receive EVT, treatment with IVT directed by individuals with expertise in thrombolytic stroke care may be beneficial to improve functional outcomes.

recommendations.title: IVT in adults with sickle cell disease
recommendations.text: In eligible adult patients with AIS with known sickle cell disease, IVT can be beneficial to improve functional outcome without increased sICH, life-threatening systemic hemorrhage, or other thrombolytic complications.

recommendations.title: IVT for non-arteritic CRAO is uncertain
recommendations.text: In adults with acute nonarteritic central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) causing disabling visual loss, and who are otherwise eligible for IVT, the usefulness of treatment with IVT within 4.5 hours of time last known well is uncertain.
recommendations.key_point: Requires ophthalmology assessment, ESR for giant cell arteritis exclusion, and shared decision-making.

recommendations.title: Don't skip IVT in patients eligible for both IVT and EVT
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS who are eligible for both IVT and EVT, IVT is safe and recommended to improve overall reperfusion efficacy and clinical outcomes.
applies_when: eligible for both IVT and EVT
recommendations.key_point: Skipping IVT to facilitate EVT is not recommended.

recommendations.title: Don't delay EVT to assess IVT response
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS who are eligible for both IVT and EVT, IVT should be administered as rapidly as possible, without observation, to assess clinical response or delay in initiating EVT, to improve treatment times and clinical outcomes.
applies_when: eligible for both IVT and EVT
recommendations.key_point: Don't observe for IVT response before EVT — every minute counts.

recommendations.title: EVT 0–6h, ASPECTS 3–10, anterior circulation LVO
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS from anterior circulation proximal LVO of the ICA or M1, presenting within 6 hours from onset of symptoms, with NIHSS score ≥6, prestroke mRS score of 0 to 1, and ASPECTS 3 to 10, EVT is recommended to improve functional clinical outcomes and reduce mortality.

recommendations.title: EVT 6–24h, ASPECTS 6–10, anterior circulation LVO
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS from anterior circulation proximal LVO of the ICA or M1 presenting between 6 and 24 hours from onset of symptoms, with NIHSS score ≥6, prestroke mRS score 0 to 1 and ASPECTS ≥6, EVT is recommended to improve functional clinical outcomes and reduce mortality.

recommendations.title: EVT 6–24h, ASPECTS 3–5, selected patients <80
recommendations.text: In selected patients with AIS from anterior circulation proximal LVO of the ICA or M1, presenting between 6 and 24 hours from onset of symptoms, with age <80 years, NIHSS score ≥6, prestroke mRS score 0 to 1, ASPECTS 3 to 5, and without significant mass effect on imaging, EVT is recommended to improve functional clinical outcomes and reduce mortality.
recommendations.key_point: Limited generalizability — patients >80, with renal failure, refractory HTN, comorbid conditions confounding neuro assessment, or life expectancy <3 months were under-represented.

recommendations.title: EVT 0–6h, ASPECTS 0–2, selected patients <80
recommendations.text: In selected patients with AIS from anterior circulation proximal LVO of the ICA or M1 presenting within 6 hours from onset of symptoms, with age <80 years, NIHSS score ≥6, prestroke mRS 0 to 1, ASPECTS 0 to 2, and without significant mass effect on imaging, EVT is reasonable to improve functional clinical outcomes and reduce mortality.

recommendations.title: EVT 0–6h with mild preexisting disability (mRS 2)
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS from anterior circulation proximal LVO of the ICA or M1 presenting within 6 hours from onset of symptoms, with NIHSS score ≥6, and ASPECTS ≥6, who have a prestroke mRS score of 2, EVT is reasonable to improve functional clinical outcomes and reduce accumulated disability.

recommendations.title: EVT 0–6h with moderate preexisting disability (mRS 3–4)
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS from anterior circulation proximal LVO of the ICA or M1 presenting within 6 hours from onset of symptoms, with NIHSS score ≥6, and ASPECTS of ≥6, who have a prestroke mRS score of 3 to 4, EVT might be reasonable to improve functional clinical outcomes and reduce accumulated disability.
recommendations.key_point: Shared decision-making with patient/family is essential given lower magnitude of effect.

recommendations.title: EVT 0–6h for dominant proximal M2 occlusion
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS from occlusion of the dominant proximal M2 division of the MCA presenting within 6 hours from onset of symptoms with a prestroke mRS score of 0 to 1, NIHSS score of ≥6, and ASPECTS of ≥6, EVT is reasonable to improve functional outcomes, but the benefits are uncertain.
recommendations.key_point: Dominant M2 = M2 segment supplying ≥50% of MCA territory (not left/right side dominance).

recommendations.title: EVT NOT recommended for nondominant M2, distal MCA, ACA, or PCA occlusions
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS from occlusion of the proximal nondominant or codominant division proximal M2 segment of the MCA, or distal MCA, anterior cerebral artery (ACA), or posterior cerebral artery (PCA), EVT is not recommended to improve functional outcomes.
recommendations.key_point: Recent ESCAPE-MeVO and DISTAL trials showed no EVT benefit for medium/distal vessel occlusions.

recommendations.title: Basilar EVT within 24h, NIHSS ≥10, PC-ASPECTS ≥6
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS, with basilar artery occlusion, a baseline mRS score of 0 to 1, NIHSS score ≥10 at presentation, and PC-ASPECTS ≥6 (mild ischemic damage), EVT within 24 hours from onset of symptoms is recommended to achieve better functional outcome and reduce mortality.
recommendations.key_point: PC-ASPECTS: pons/midbrain (2 pts each), thalami (1 each), occipital lobes (1 each), cerebellar hemispheres (1 each).

recommendations.title: Basilar EVT with NIHSS 6–9 — effectiveness not well established
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS, with basilar artery occlusion, a baseline mRS score of 0 to 1, NIHSS score 6 to 9 at presentation, and PC-ASPECTS ≥6 (mild ischemic damage) the effectiveness of EVT within 24 hours to improve functional outcomes and reduce mortality is not well established.

recommendations.title: Correct hypotension and hypovolemia
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS, hypotension and hypovolemia should be corrected to maintain systemic perfusion levels necessary to support organ function.

recommendations.title: Treat HTN early when comorbid conditions require it
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS, early treatment of hypertension is indicated when required by comorbid conditions (eg, concomitant acute coronary event, acute heart failure, aortic dissection, postthrombolysis sICH, or preeclampsia/eclampsia) to reduce the risk of complications.

recommendations.title: Lower BP <185/110 before IVT
recommendations.text: Patients with AIS who have elevated BP and are otherwise eligible for treatment with IVT should have their SBP lowered to <185 mm Hg and diastolic blood pressure (DBP) <110 mm Hg before IVT therapy is initiated to reduce hemorrhagic complications.
recommendations.key_point: Target: SBP <185 mm Hg AND DBP <110 mm Hg before starting IVT.

recommendations.title: Pre-EVT BP target ≤185/110 (no IVT given)
recommendations.text: In patients for whom EVT is planned and who have not received IVT therapy, it is reasonable to maintain BP ≤185/110 mm Hg before the procedure to avoid complications and improve patient outcomes.

recommendations.title: Maintain BP <180/105 for 24h after IVT
recommendations.text: BP should be maintained at <180/105 mm Hg for at least the first 24 hours after IVT treatment.
recommendations.key_point: Target: <180/105 mm Hg for ≥24 hours post-IVT.

recommendations.title: Intensive SBP <140 after IVT in mild–moderate AIS not recommended
recommendations.text: In patients with mild to moderate severity AIS who have been treated with IVT, intensive SBP reduction (target of <140 mm Hg compared with <180 mm Hg) is not recommended because it is not associated with an improvement in functional outcome.

recommendations.title: Maintain BP ≤180/105 during and 24h after EVT
recommendations.text: In patients who undergo EVT, it is reasonable to maintain BP at a level ≤180/105 mm Hg during and for 24 hours after the procedure.

recommendations.title: Intensive SBP <140 after successful EVT recanalization is HARMFUL
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS with LVO of the anterior circulation who have been successfully recanalized by endovascular therapy (mTICI 2b, 2c, or 3) and without other indication for blood pressure management target, intensive SBP reduction target of <140 mm Hg for the first 72 hours is harmful and not recommended.
recommendations.key_point: Do NOT target SBP <140 mm Hg in the first 72h after successful EVT — this causes harm (lower functional independence, higher mortality).

recommendations.title: Routine BP lowering 48–72h after AIS not effective (no IVT/EVT)
recommendations.text: In patients with BP <220/120 mm Hg who did not receive IVT or EVT and do not have a comorbid condition requiring urgent antihypertensive treatment, initiating or reinitiating treatment of hypertension within the first 48 to 72 hours after an AIS is not effective to prevent death or dependency.

recommendations.title: Treat hypoglycemia <60 mg/dL
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS, hypoglycemia (blood glucose <60 mg/dL) should be treated to avoid complications.
recommendations.key_point: Hypoglycemia can mimic stroke; J-shaped relationship with mortality.

recommendations.title: Treat persistent hyperglycemia to target 140–180 mg/dL
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS, it is reasonable to treat persistent hyperglycemia to achieve blood glucose levels in a range of 140 to 180 mg/dL with close monitoring to prevent worse functional outcomes.
recommendations.key_point: Target 140–180 mg/dL — not tighter.

recommendations.title: Tight glucose control (80–130) NOT recommended
recommendations.text: In hospitalized patients with AIS with hyperglycemia, treatment with IV insulin to achieve blood glucose levels in the range of 80 to 130 mg/dL is not recommended to improve 3-month functional outcomes.
recommendations.key_point: SHINE trial: tight control causes hypoglycemia without functional benefit.

recommendations.title: Aspirin within 48 hours of AIS
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS, administration of aspirin is recommended within 48 hours after stroke onset to reduce risk of death and dependency.

recommendations.title: DAPT for 21 days in minor noncardioembolic AIS or high-risk TIA (no IVT)
recommendations.text: In patients with minor (NIHSS score ≤3) noncardioembolic AIS or high-risk TIA (ABCD2 score ≥4) who did not receive IVT, DAPT (aspirin and clopidogrel with loading dose of clopidogrel) should be initiated early (within 24 hours after symptom onset) and continued for 21 days, followed by single antiplatelet therapy (SAPT) to reduce the 90-day risk of recurrent ischemic stroke.
recommendations.key_point: NIHSS ≤3 (not ≤5) — load clopidogrel, start within 24h, 21 days then SAPT.

recommendations.title: Don't substitute aspirin for IVT/EVT
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS who are otherwise eligible for IVT or mechanical thrombectomy, aspirin is not recommended as a substitute for acute stroke treatment to improve patient outcomes.
recommendations.key_point: Aspirin alone is not a substitute for thrombolysis or thrombectomy when patient is eligible.

recommendations.title: Don't give IV aspirin within 90 minutes of IVT start
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS who are eligible for IVT, IV aspirin should not be administered concurrently or within 90 minutes after the start of IVT given the risk of hemorrhage.

recommendations.title: Early oral anticoagulation in AF after milder AIS is reasonable
recommendations.text: In carefully selected (eg, milder severity) patients with AIS with atrial fibrillation, a strategy of early oral anticoagulation poststroke is low risk and is reasonable compared with a strategy of delayed anticoagulation, although the efficacy of early anticoagulation for prevention of early recurrent stroke is not established.
recommendations.key_point: ELAN, OPTIMAS, TIMING trials: early DOAC (≤4 days) noninferior to delayed.

recommendations.title: Early anticoagulation within 48h is not recommended
recommendations.text: In patients with AIS, early anticoagulation (within 48 hours of stroke onset) does not reduce the likelihood of early neurological worsening or increase the likelihood of a favorable functional outcome and is not recommended.
recommendations.key_point: Theoretical benefits of preventing recurrence/worsening have not been borne out in trials.

recommendations.title: MSUs over conventional EMS for thrombolytic-eligible patients
recommendations.text: In patients with suspected AIS, the use of MSUs over conventional EMS where available is recommended for the transport and management of thrombolytic-eligible patients to ensure the fastest achievable onset-to-treatment time and improve functional outcomes.

recommendations.title: MSUs for EVT triage to thrombectomy-capable facility
recommendations.text: In endovascular thrombectomy-eligible patients, use of MSUs can be beneficial to identify and triage patients to the appropriate thrombectomy-capable facility with prehospital notification of receiving stroke teams.

recommendations.title: Telestroke for hospitals without acute neurological expertise
recommendations.text: For patients with AIS presenting to EDs without acute neurological expertise, telestroke systems are effective over usual care by the ED team for IVT decision-making and optimal thrombolytic delivery including increased thrombolytic administration and shorter time to delivery.

recommendations.title: Telestroke reduces short-term mortality
recommendations.text: For patients with AIS treated at hospitals without local stroke expertise, telestroke is reasonable to reduce short-term mortality.

recommendations.title: Prehospital telemedicine in ambulance
recommendations.text: For patients with suspected stroke in the prehospital setting, telemedicine in the ambulance should be considered, when feasible, to complement paramedic assessment to identify candidates for reperfusion interventions.

recommendations.title: Emergent MRI/MRA preferred for pediatric AIS workup
recommendations.text: In pediatric patients with suspected AIS, emergent brain and vascular imaging with MRI/MRA of the cervical and intracranial vessels is reasonable to identify patients with large vessel occlusion and to differentiate arterial ischemic stroke from hemorrhagic stroke or stroke mimics.
recommendations.key_point: MRI/MRA preferred when feasible without delay; better differentiates AIS from mimics in children.

recommendations.title: CT/CTA acceptable when pediatric MRI not available within 25 min
recommendations.text: In pediatric patients with suspected AIS, emergent brain and vascular imaging with CT/CTA of the cervical and intracranial vessels is reasonable if MRI/MRA imaging is not available immediately (within 25 minutes) to identify patients with large vessel occlusion.

recommendations.title: IV alteplase may be considered in pediatric patients 28d–18y within 4.5h
recommendations.text: In pediatric patients aged 28 days to 18 years with confirmed AIS presenting within 4.5 hours of symptom onset and disabling deficits, IVT with alteplase may be considered as it is safe, but efficacy is uncertain.
recommendations.key_point: TIPS trial stopped due to recruitment but confirmed safety of 0.9 mg/kg alteplase in pediatric patients with PedNIHSS ≥4. Tenecteplase pediatric data are limited (n=11).

recommendations.title: EVT for pediatric ≥6 years with LVO within 6h of onset
recommendations.text: In pediatric patients ≥6 years with acute neurological symptoms and ischemic stroke due to LVO and within 6 hours from symptom onset, EVT can be effective if performed by experienced neurointerventionalists to improve functional outcomes.
recommendations.key_point: Save ChildS Pro registry: better outcomes with EVT vs medical alone, especially ≥6y, <6h, PedNIHSS ≥6.

recommendations.title: EVT for pediatric ≥6 years with LVO 6–24h with salvageable tissue
recommendations.text: In pediatric patients ≥6 years with acute neurological symptoms and ischemic stroke due to LVO, 6 to 24 hours from symptom onset, and with potentially salvageable brain tissue, EVT can be effective to improve functional outcomes.
recommendations.key_point: Pediatric perfusion thresholds for penumbra/core not yet established — adult thresholds may not apply.

recommendations.title: EVT for pediatric 28d–6y with LVO within 24h may be reasonable
recommendations.text: In pediatric patients aged 28 days to 6 years with acute neurological symptoms, including first-time seizure and AIS due to LVO, within 24 hours from symptom onset, and with potentially salvageable brain tissue, EVT performed by neurointerventionalists with pediatric experience may be reasonable to improve functional outcomes.
recommendations.key_point: Smaller vessel caliber in <6y requires highly skilled pediatric neurointerventionalist. Neonates (<28d) — only case reports, no systematic evidence.

ivt_eligibility_checklist.title: IV Thrombolytic Eligibility — Quick Reference
ivt_eligibility_checklist.preamble: Use as a structured reminder of the AHA-aligned considerations. Final eligibility is the treating clinician's decision.

items.question: Within 4.5 hours of last known well (LKW)?
Wake-up or unknown onset, with DWI-FLAIR mismatch on MRI?
4.5–9h with salvageable penumbra on perfusion imaging?
Disabling neurological deficit? (Use disabling-deficit framework, not NIHSS alone — see Table 4)
BP can be lowered to <185/110 mm Hg before bolus?
Blood glucose checked? (Severe hypo/hyperglycemia treated and corrected)
Mild–moderate early ischemic changes are NOT a contraindication
No known bleeding diathesis (platelets <100k, INR >1.7, aPTT >40s, recent therapeutic LMWH)?
No DOAC dose within 48h (with normal renal function)?
Single/dual antiplatelet use is NOT a contraindication (COR 1, B-NR)
No major surgery in past 14 days?
No serious head trauma or prior stroke in past 3 months?

categories.name: Special populations (consider individually — not absolute exclusions)

items.question: Sickle cell disease — IVT can be beneficial in adults (COR 2a, B-NR; pediatric SCD risk-benefit unknown).
Pregnancy — individualized risk-benefit; IVT is not absolutely contraindicated.
Mild non-disabling deficits (NIHSS ≤3) — IVT NOT recommended; DAPT preferred (COR 3 No Benefit, B-R).
